And to that I rejoin: -- O my father, did you not wish me to live as happily as possible? And yet you also never ceased telling me that I should live as justly as possible. Now, here the giver of the rule, whether he be legislator or father, will be in a dilemma, and will in vain endeavour to be consistent with himself."

"But if he were to declare that the justest life is also the happiest, every one hearing him would enquire, if I am not mistaken, what is that good and noble principle in life which the law approves, and which is superior to pleasure. For what good can the just man have which is separated from pleasure? Shall we say that glory and fame, coming from Gods and men, though good and noble, are nevertheless unpleasant, and infamy pleasant? Certainly not, sweet legislator. Or shall we say that the not-doing of wrong and there being no wrong done is good and honourable, although there is no pleasure in it, and that the doing wrong is pleasant, but evil and base?"

"Impossible."
refashioned:
"These words are better suited to fathers and legislators, so let me pose my questions to one of them. Suppose a father tells his child that the pleasantest life is the happiest. The child would naturally reply: 'Father, didn't you want me to live as happily as possible? Yet you always told me to live as justly as possible.' The father -- or legislator -- would find himself in a dilemma, unable to be consistent."

"But suppose instead he declares that the most just life is also the happiest. Everyone would then ask: what is the good and noble principle the law approves that is supposedly superior to pleasure? What good does the just person possess apart from pleasure? Should we say that glory from gods and humans, though good and noble, is actually unpleasant, while infamy is pleasant? Certainly not. Should we say that refraining from wrongdoing is good and honorable but brings no pleasure, while committing wrong is pleasant but evil?"

"Impossible."
elegant: "A father who separates justice from pleasure faces a dilemma: he tells his child to be happy and to be just, but these pull in different directions. A father who identifies them faces a different challenge: what good does the law approve that surpasses pleasure? Is glory unpleasant? Is innocence joyless? Is wrongdoing pleasant? None of these can be maintained."
summary: The Athenian shows that separating justice from pleasure creates an impossible dilemma for fathers and legislators: they cannot consistently urge both happiness and justice. But identifying them raises the question of what good justice offers beyond pleasure -- which leads to the conclusion that justice must itself be pleasant.
distilled: Any father or legislator who separates justice from pleasure will be unable to give consistent advice about how to live well.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The father-child dialogue is a rhetorical device that personalizes the abstract philosophical argument. The dilemma is genuinely powerful: if justice and pleasure diverge, moral education collapses into contradiction. The series of rhetorical questions (is glory unpleasant? is innocence joyless?) systematically eliminates ways to separate justice from pleasure. Cross-reference: Republic 2.362e-367e where Adeimantus describes how fathers give contradictory advice about justice; Protagoras 351b-360e on the identity of the good and the pleasant; Philebus 20d-22e on whether pleasure or wisdom constitutes the good life.

narrative: "The view which identifies the pleasant and the just and the good and the noble has an excellent moral and religious tendency. And the opposite view is most at variance with the designs of the legislator, and is, in his opinion, infamous; for no one, if he can help, will be persuaded to do that which gives him more pain than pleasure. But as distant prospects are apt to make us dizzy, especially in childhood, the legislator will try to purge away the darkness and exhibit the truth; he will persuade the citizens, in some way or other, by customs and praises and words, that just and unjust are shadows only, and that injustice, which seems opposed to justice, when contemplated by the unjust and evil man appears pleasant and the just most unpleasant; but that from the just man's point of view, the very opposite is the appearance of both of them."
refashioned:
"The view that identifies the pleasant with the just, the good, and the noble has excellent moral and religious consequences. The opposite view -- separating pleasure from justice -- directly opposes the legislator's purpose and is, in his judgment, disgraceful. No one will willingly do what brings more pain than pleasure."

"But distant outcomes tend to distort our vision, especially in childhood. So the legislator will try to dispel the darkness and reveal the truth. Through customs, praise, and teaching, he will persuade citizens that our impressions of justice and injustice are mere shadows: to the unjust person, injustice appears pleasant and justice most unpleasant, but from the just person's perspective, exactly the opposite is true."
elegant: "Identifying the pleasant with the just serves both morality and religion. The opposite view undermines legislation, since no one willingly chooses pain over pleasure. But perspective distorts judgment, especially in youth. The legislator must reveal that our views of justice and injustice are shadows: to the unjust, injustice seems pleasant; to the just, the opposite is true."
summary: The Athenian argues that identifying pleasure with justice is essential for legislation, since no one willingly chooses pain. The legislator must show that our judgments about justice are like shadows -- the unjust person perceives injustice as pleasant only because of a distorted perspective.
distilled: The legislator must reveal that the apparent pleasantness of injustice is a shadow cast by moral distortion, not a true perception.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The imagery of shadows and distorted perspective directly evokes the Cave allegory of Republic 7.514a-517a, though here applied to moral rather than metaphysical perception. The claim that 'just and unjust are shadows only' means our unreflective impressions of them are shadowy, not that justice itself is unreal. The legislator's role as one who 'purges darkness' parallels the philosopher's role in the Republic. Cross-reference: Republic 7.514a-517a (Cave allegory); Republic 9.583b-585a on the illusory pleasures of the unjust; Protagoras 356c-357b on the art of measurement that overcomes the distortion of distance; Philebus 41e-42c on false pleasures.

narrative:
"True."

"And which may be supposed to be the truer judgment -- that of the inferior or of the better soul?"

"Surely, that of the better soul."

"Then the unjust life must not only be more base and depraved, but also more unpleasant than the just and holy life?"

"That seems to be implied in the present argument."

"And even supposing this were otherwise, and not as the argument has proven, still the lawgiver, who is worth anything, if he ever ventures to tell a lie to the young for their good, could not invent a more useful lie than this, or one which will have a better effect in making them do what is right, not on compulsion but voluntarily."

"Truth, Stranger, is a noble thing and a lasting, but a thing of which men are hard to be persuaded."

"And yet the story of the Sidonian Cadmus, which is so improbable, has been readily believed, and also innumerable other tales."

"What is that story?"
refashioned:
"True."

"And whose judgment should we trust more -- that of the inferior soul or the better soul?"

"The better soul's, surely."

"Then the unjust life must be not only more base and depraved, but also more unpleasant than the just and holy life?"

"That does seem to follow from our argument."

"And even if this were not the case -- even if the argument had not proven it -- a legislator worth anything, if he ever dared tell a lie to the young for their benefit, could not invent a more useful lie than this one. No falsehood would be more effective at making people do what is right voluntarily rather than under compulsion."

"Truth, Stranger, is a noble and enduring thing -- but people are very hard to persuade of it."

"And yet the story of the Sidonian Cadmus, which is wildly improbable, has been readily believed, along with countless other tales."

"What story is that?"
elegant:
"Whose judgment is truer -- the inferior soul's or the better soul's? The better soul's. Therefore the unjust life is both more base and more unpleasant than the just life. Even if this weren't true, no legislator could invent a more useful belief for making people do right voluntarily."

"Truth is noble but hard to believe."

"Yet people readily believe the improbable story of Cadmus and his sown teeth."
summary: The argument concludes that since the better soul judges more truly, the unjust life is genuinely more unpleasant. The Athenian adds that even if this were merely a useful fiction, no better one exists for promoting voluntary virtue. Cleinias objects that truth is hard to accept, and the Athenian counters that people readily believe far more improbable myths.
distilled: The better soul judges more truly, so the unjust life truly is more unpleasant; and even as a 'noble lie,' no more useful belief exists for promoting virtue.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The passage raises the profound question of the 'noble lie' (cf. Republic 3.414b-415d) -- the Stranger suggests that even if the identity of justice and happiness were false, it would still be the most useful belief for a legislator to promote. This pragmatic approach to truth is distinctive to the Laws. Cleinias's remark about truth being 'noble but hard to believe' is one of his most memorable lines. The reference to Cadmus, who founded Thebes by sowing dragon's teeth from which armed men sprang, illustrates the power of myth over reason. Cross-reference: Republic 3.414b-415d on the 'noble lie'; Republic 2.382c-d on beneficial falsehoods; Critias 110a on ancient myths as vehicles of truth.

narrative:
"The story of armed men springing up after the sowing of teeth, which the legislator may take as a proof that he can persuade the minds of the young of anything; so that he has only to reflect and find out what belief will be of the greatest public advantage, and then use all his efforts to make the whole community utter one and the same word in their songs and tales and discourses all their life long. But if you do not agree with me, there is no reason why you should not argue on the other side."

"I do not see that any argument can fairly be raised by either of us against what you are now saying."
refashioned:
"The story of armed warriors springing from sown teeth. The legislator can take this as evidence that the minds of the young can be persuaded of anything. He need only determine which belief will bring the greatest public benefit, and then devote all his efforts to making the entire community express one and the same message in their songs, stories, and conversations throughout their lives. But if you disagree with me, you are free to argue the other side."

"I do not see how either of us could fairly raise an argument against what you are now saying."
elegant:
"If people believe that armed warriors sprang from sown teeth, a legislator can persuade the young of anything. He need only discover the most beneficial belief and ensure the whole community repeats it in songs, stories, and discourse throughout life."

"I cannot argue against that."
summary: The Athenian draws a practical lesson from the Cadmus myth: if people will believe something so improbable, a legislator can surely persuade the young of the truth that justice equals happiness. The entire community should reinforce this message consistently through all forms of expression.
distilled: If people believe the myth of Cadmus, the legislator can persuade them of anything, and should use this power to promote the belief that justice is happiness.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The argument from the Cadmus myth to legislative persuasion is both pragmatic and unsettling -- it acknowledges the manipulability of human belief while directing it toward what the Stranger considers truth. The vision of an entire community 'uttering one and the same word' in all their cultural expressions represents total cultural coordination under legislative authority. Cross-reference: Republic 3.414b-415d on the 'noble lie' of the metals myth; Republic 2.377b-378e on controlling stories told to children; Laws 7.811c-e on selecting poems for civic education. The Stranger's invitation to disagree ('argue on the other side') shows a dialogical openness that softens the authoritarian implications.

narrative:
"The next suggestion which I have to offer is, that all our three choruses shall sing to the young and tender souls of children, reciting in their strains all the noble thoughts of which we have already spoken, or are about to speak; and the sum of them shall be, that the life which is by the Gods deemed to be the happiest is also the best; -- we shall affirm this to be a most certain truth; and the minds of our young disciples will be more likely to receive these words of ours than any others which we might address to them."

"I assent to what you say."
refashioned:
"My next proposal is this: all three of our choruses should sing to the young and impressionable souls of children, conveying in their songs all the noble ideas we have discussed or will discuss. The central message shall be that the life the gods consider happiest is also the best. We will declare this to be absolute truth. And the minds of young people will be more receptive to this message than to any other we could give them."

"I agree."
elegant: "All three choruses should sing to children one central truth: the life the gods deem happiest is also the best. Young minds will receive this message more readily than any other."
summary: The Athenian proposes that all three choruses should convey a single core message to children: the life the gods consider happiest is also the most virtuous. He argues young minds are especially receptive to this teaching.
distilled: The three choruses must unite in teaching children that the divinely approved happiest life is also the best and most virtuous life.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The 'three choruses' are formally introduced here and elaborated in 2.31 -- children, young adults, and elders. The theological grounding (what the gods deem happiest) gives the educational message divine authority. The emphasis on children's receptivity connects to the earlier argument (2.2) about education as pre-rational habituation. Cross-reference: Laws 7.811c-812a on selecting appropriate songs for children; Republic 2.377a-b on the importance of the first stories children hear; Laws 2.31 for the detailed structure of the three choruses.

narrative:
"First will enter in their natural order the sacred choir composed of children, which is to sing lustily the heaven-taught lay to the whole city. Next will follow the choir of young men under the age of thirty, who will call upon the God Paean to testify to the truth of their words, and will pray him to be gracious to the youth and to turn their hearts. Thirdly, the choir of elder men, who are from thirty to sixty years of age, will also sing. There remain those who are too old to sing, and they will tell stories, illustrating the same virtues, as with the voice of an oracle."

"Who are those who compose the third choir, Stranger? for I do not clearly understand what you mean to say about them."

"And yet almost all that I have been saying has been said with a view to them."

"Will you try to be a little plainer?"
refashioned:
"First, in their natural order, comes the sacred children's choir, which will sing the divinely inspired hymn vigorously to the whole city. Next comes the choir of young men under thirty, who will invoke the god Paean to witness the truth of their words and pray for his grace to turn the hearts of the youth. Third comes the choir of elder men, aged thirty to sixty, who will also sing. Those too old to sing will remain and tell stories illustrating the same virtues, speaking with the authority of an oracle."

"Who exactly makes up this third choir, Stranger? I do not fully understand what you are saying about them."

"And yet almost everything I have been discussing has been said with them specifically in mind."

"Could you try to be a bit clearer?"
elegant:
"Three choruses will perform in order: first, children singing the sacred hymn to the city; second, young men under thirty invoking Paean as witness; third, elders aged thirty to sixty. Those too old to sing will tell stories with oracular authority, illustrating the same virtues."

"Who is the third chorus? I don't fully understand."

"Nearly everything I've said has been directed at them."
summary: The Athenian describes three choruses organized by age: children, young men under thirty (invoking Paean), and elders aged thirty to sixty, with the very oldest serving as oracular storytellers. Cleinias is confused about the third chorus, prompting the Athenian to note that much of the preceding discussion was aimed at explaining their role.
distilled: Three age-graded choruses -- children, young adults, and elders -- will perform the city's sacred educational songs, with the very oldest serving as storytellers.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, with Cleinias and an implied question. The three-chorus structure is the institutional backbone of the Laws' educational system. Paean (or Paian) is Apollo in his capacity as healer -- the young men's invocation connects music to healing. The elders who are 'too old to sing' but tell stories 'with the voice of an oracle' represent the highest authority -- their wisdom transcends the performative requirements of the younger choruses. Cleinias's confusion is dramatically important: it prompts the extended discussion of the Dionysian chorus (the third chorus of elders) that follows. Cross-reference: Laws 7.799a-800a on choral regulations; Republic 3.398c-399e on appropriate musical modes for different ages; Laws 6.765a on the role of elder citizens.

narrative: "I was speaking at the commencement of our discourse, as you will remember, of the fiery nature of young creatures: I said that they were unable to keep quiet either in limb or voice, and that they called out and jumped about in a disorderly manner; and that no other animal attained to any perception of order, but man only. Now the order of motion is called rhythm, and the order of the voice, in which high and low are duly mingled, is called harmony; and both together are termed choric song. And I said that the Gods had pity on us, and gave us Apollo and the Muses to be our playfellows and leaders in the dance; and Dionysus, as I dare say that you will remember, was the third."
refashioned: "As you will recall, at the beginning of our discussion I spoke of the fiery nature of young creatures -- how they cannot keep their bodies or voices still, but cry out and jump about in a disorderly way. No other animal achieves any sense of order in its movements; only humans do. The order of bodily motion is called rhythm; the order of the voice, in which high and low are properly blended, is called harmony; and the two combined are called choral song. I also said that the gods, pitying our condition, gave us Apollo and the Muses as companions and leaders in the dance -- and Dionysus, as you will surely remember, was the third."
elegant: "Recall our starting point: young creatures move and cry without order. Only humans perceive rhythm -- order in motion -- and harmony -- order in voice. Together these form choral song. The gods gave us Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus as companions in this education through dance."
summary: The Athenian recapitulates the earlier argument: young creatures move chaotically, but humans uniquely perceive rhythm and harmony, which together constitute choral song. He reminds his listeners that the gods gave Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus as guides for this musical education.
distilled: Humans alone perceive rhythm and harmony, which together form choral song -- a divine gift through Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is a deliberate recapitulation of the argument from 2.3, linking back before moving forward to the Dionysian chorus. The definitions of rhythm (order of motion) and harmony (order of voice) are foundational to Greek musical theory. The triad of Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus maps onto the three choruses: Apollo for children, the Muses for young adults, and Dionysus for elders. Cross-reference: Laws 2.3 (the original statement); Republic 3.399e-400d on rhythm and harmony; Aristoxenus, Elements of Rhythm for the technical development of these concepts.

narrative:
"I quite remember."

"Thus far I have spoken of the chorus of Apollo and the Muses, and I have still to speak of the remaining chorus, which is that of Dionysus."

"How is that arranged? There is something strange, at any rate on first hearing, in a Dionysiac chorus of old men, if you really mean that those who are above thirty, and may be fifty, or from fifty to sixty years of age, are to dance in his honour."

"Very true; and therefore it must be shown that there is good reason for the proposal."

"Certainly."

"Are we agreed thus far?"

"About what?"

"That every man and boy, slave and free, both sexes, and the whole city, should never cease charming themselves with the strains of which we have spoken; and that there should be every sort of change and variation of them in order to take away the effect of sameness, so that the singers may always receive pleasure from their hymns, and may never weary of them?"

"Every one will agree."

"Where, then, will that best part of our city which, by reason of age and intelligence, has the greatest influence, sing these fairest of strains, which are to do so much good? Shall we be so foolish as to let them off who would give us the most beautiful and also the most useful of songs?"

"But, says the argument, we cannot let them off."

"Then how can we carry out our purpose with decorum? Will this be the way?"

"What?"

"When a man is advancing in years, he is afraid and reluctant to sing; -- he has no pleasure in his own performances; and if compulsion is used, he will be more and more ashamed, the older and more discreet he grows; -- is not this true?"

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"I remember."

"So far I have discussed the choruses of Apollo and the Muses. I still need to address the remaining chorus -- that of Dionysus."

"How is it organized? It sounds strange, at least initially, to have a Dionysiac chorus of old men -- people over thirty, perhaps fifty, or even fifty to sixty years old, dancing in his honor."

"Quite true, which is why we must demonstrate good reason for the proposal."

"Of course."

"Do we agree on this much: that everyone in the city -- men and boys, slaves and free, both sexes -- should continually immerse themselves in the musical strains we have described, with enough variety and change to prevent monotony, so that the singers always find fresh pleasure in their hymns?"

"Everyone would agree with that."

"Then where will the best citizens -- those with the greatest influence due to age and wisdom -- sing these finest and most beneficial strains? Shall we foolishly excuse the very people who could give us the most beautiful and useful songs?"

"The logic demands we cannot excuse them."

"But how do we accomplish this with propriety? Consider the problem: as a person grows older, he becomes afraid and reluctant to sing. He takes no pleasure in his own performances, and if forced, he feels increasingly ashamed the older and more dignified he becomes. Is this not true?"

"It certainly is."
elegant: "The chorus of Dionysus remains to be discussed. A chorus of old men dancing for Dionysus sounds strange, so we need good reason. We agree the whole city should sing unceasingly with enough variety to prevent boredom. The wisest elders should sing the finest songs. But the problem: older men are ashamed and reluctant to perform, growing more so with age and dignity."
summary: The Athenian introduces the chorus of Dionysus -- a chorus of elders -- acknowledging it sounds strange. He establishes that the wisest citizens should sing the most important songs, but identifies the practical problem: older people are ashamed and reluctant to perform publicly.
distilled: The wisest elders should sing the most important songs, but their shame and reluctance to perform must be overcome.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The Dionysian chorus of elders is the Laws' most innovative institutional proposal. The association of Dionysus with older performers inverts the usual connection of Dionysus with youthful revelry. The practical problem of elders' performance anxiety is treated with genuine psychological insight. The principle that variety prevents boredom shows attention to the sustainability of cultural institutions. Cross-reference: Laws 1.641b-c on the Spartan prohibition against Dionysiac festivals; Euripides, Bacchae 170-214 where elderly Cadmus and Tiresias join the Dionysiac dance; Republic 3.395d-e on the indignity of imitation for serious men.

narrative:
"Well, and will he not be yet more ashamed if he has to stand up and sing in the theatre to a mixed audience? -- and if moreover when he is required to do so, like the other choirs who contend for prizes, and have been trained under a singing master, he is pinched and hungry, he will certainly have a feeling of shame and discomfort which will make him very unwilling to exhibit."

"No doubt."
refashioned:
"And would he not feel even greater shame if required to stand and sing in a theatre before a mixed audience? Furthermore, if he must perform like the other competitive choirs -- trained under a choirmaster, physically strained and underfed from the demanding regimen -- he will certainly feel such shame and discomfort that he becomes extremely unwilling to perform."

"No doubt about it."
elegant: "The shame intensifies if the elder must perform in a theatre before a mixed audience, and worse still if subjected to the same competitive training regimen -- physically depleted and hunger-weakened -- as younger choruses. He would be completely unwilling."
summary: The Athenian intensifies the problem: elders would feel even more ashamed performing in a theatre before mixed audiences, especially if subjected to the rigorous and physically depleting training regimen of competitive choruses.
distilled: Elders forced into competitive theatrical performance under harsh training conditions would be overcome by shame and unwillingness.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The detail about being 'pinched and hungry' refers to the strict physical regimen that competitive chorus members underwent before dramatic festivals. The Laws distinguishes its educational choruses from the competitive Athenian model, where choruses trained intensively for prizes at festivals like the Dionysia. Cross-reference: Antiphon 6 (On the Choreutes) for the practical realities of chorus training in Athens; Aristophanes, Frogs 686-705 for comic depictions of chorus training; Laws 2.35 where wine is proposed as the solution to this problem.

narrative:
"How, then, shall we reassure him, and get him to sing? Shall we begin by enacting that boys shall not taste wine at all until they are eighteen years of age; we will tell them that fire must not be poured upon fire, whether in the body or in the soul, until they begin to go to work -- this is a precaution which has to be taken against the excitableness of youth; -- afterwards they may taste wine in moderation up to the age of thirty, but while a man is young he should abstain altogether from intoxication and from excess of wine; when, at length, he has reached forty years, after dinner at a public mess, he may invite not only the other Gods, but Dionysus above all, to the mystery and festivity of the elder men, making use of the wine which he has given men to lighten the sourness of old age; that in age we may renew our youth, and forget our sorrows; and also in order that the nature of the soul, like iron melted in the fire, may become softer and so more impressible."

"In the first place, will not any one who is thus mellowed be more ready and less ashamed to sing -- I do not say before a large audience, but before a moderate company; nor yet among strangers, but among his familiars, and, as we have often said, to chant, and to enchant?"
refashioned:
"How, then, do we reassure the reluctant elder and persuade him to sing? Let us begin with regulations about wine. Boys should not taste wine at all before the age of eighteen -- fire should not be poured upon fire, whether in body or soul, before they begin serious work. This is a precaution against the excitability of youth. After eighteen, they may taste wine in moderation until thirty, but while young, a person should completely avoid drunkenness and excess."

"At forty, however, after dinner at a public mess, a man may invite not only the other gods but Dionysus above all to the mystery and celebration of the elders, making use of the wine that the god gave humanity to soften the harshness of old age -- so that in our later years we may renew our youth and forget our sorrows, and so that the soul, like iron heated in fire, may become softer and more receptive."

"First of all: would not a person thus mellowed be readier and less ashamed to sing? Not before a huge audience, but before a moderate gathering; not among strangers, but among friends -- to chant, and to enchant, as we have often said?"
elegant: "To overcome elders' reluctance: no wine before eighteen (fire should not be poured on fire), moderation until thirty, and full abstinence from excess while young. But at forty, after public meals, elders may invoke Dionysus and use wine to soften the harshness of age -- renewing youth, forgetting sorrow, making the soul receptive like iron in fire. A mellowed elder will sing more willingly among friends."
summary: The Athenian proposes age-graded wine regulations: total abstinence before eighteen, moderation until thirty, and at forty, wine used ceremonially at public meals to overcome elders' reluctance to sing. Wine softens the soul like iron in fire, making older people receptive enough to participate in choral performance.
distilled: Wine, used ceremonially by those over forty, softens the hardened soul like iron in fire, enabling reluctant elders to participate in educational choral singing.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This is the passage that ties together the drinking discussion from Book 1 with the choral education program of Book 2. The metaphor of the soul as iron softened in fire is one of Plato's most striking images -- the aged soul hardens and must be made malleable again through the controlled use of wine. The age-graded regulations (no wine before 18, moderation before 30, liberty after 40) represent a pragmatic approach unusual for Plato. The wordplay on 'chant' (epaoidai) and 'enchant' (epaidein) recurs from 2.19. Cross-reference: Laws 1.645d-650b on wine as a test of character; Laws 2.19 on the enchantment of chants; Republic 3.411a-412a on the soul becoming too hard or too soft; Symposium 176a-e on drinking regulations at the symposium.

narrative:
"He will be far more ready."

"There will be no impropriety in our using such a method of persuading them to join with us in song."

"None at all."

"And what strain will they sing, and what muse will they hymn? The strain should clearly be one suitable to them."

"Certainly."

"And what strain is suitable for heroes? Shall they sing a choric strain?"

"Truly, Stranger, we of Crete and Lacedaemon know no strain other than that which we have learnt and been accustomed to sing in our chorus."
refashioned:
"He will be far more ready."

"Then there is nothing improper in using this method to persuade them to join in song."

"Nothing at all."

"What will they sing, and to what muse will they dedicate their hymns? The music should clearly be suitable for them."

"Of course."

"And what music is suitable for men of heroic character? Should they sing choral songs?"

"Honestly, Stranger, we Cretans and Spartans know no songs other than those we have learned and been accustomed to singing in our choruses."
elegant:
"Wine will make elders more willing, and there is nothing improper in this method. But what should they sing? What music suits heroic men?"

"We Cretans and Spartans know only the songs of our traditional choruses."
summary: Both agree that using wine to encourage elders' participation is proper. When asked what music suits heroic elders, Cleinias admits that Cretans and Spartans know only their traditional choral music, revealing the limitations of their military-oriented culture.
distilled: Cleinias admits that Cretan and Spartan musical knowledge is limited to traditional military choruses, lacking the refinement the Athenian seeks.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. Cleinias's admission of limited musical knowledge is dramatically significant -- it reveals the cultural narrowness that the Athenian has been gently criticizing. The Dorian states preserved ancient choral forms but did not develop the rich variety of musical modes and genres found in Athens and Ionia. The question about 'heroic' music anticipates the critique in 2.37, where the Stranger argues that true heroism requires more than military virtue. Cross-reference: Republic 3.398e-399a on the Dorian and Phrygian modes as the only appropriate ones; Laws 1.633a-b on the limitations of Spartan education; Plutarch, Life of Lycurgus 21 on Spartan musical conservatism.

narrative: "I dare say; for you have never acquired the knowledge of the most beautiful kind of song, in your military way of life, which is modelled after the camp, and is not like that of dwellers in cities; and you have your young men herding and feeding together like young colts. No one takes his own individual colt and drags him away from his fellows against his will, raging and foaming, and gives him a groom to attend to him alone, and trains and rubs him down privately, and gives him the qualities in education which will make him not only a good soldier, but also a governor of a state and of cities. Such an one, as we said at first, would be a greater warrior than he of whom Tyrtaeus sings; and he would honour courage everywhere, but always as the fourth, and not as the first part of virtue, either in individuals or states."
refashioned:
"I am not surprised. Your military way of life is modeled on the camp rather than the city, so you have never encountered the most beautiful kind of song. Your young men are herded and raised together like young colts in a herd. No one separates an individual colt from his companions -- struggling and wild -- to give him a personal groom, train him individually, groom him privately, and give him the kind of education that produces not just a good soldier but a governor of states and cities."

"Such a person, as we said at the beginning, would be a greater warrior than the one Tyrtaeus celebrates in his poems. He would honor courage everywhere, but always as the fourth part of virtue -- never the first -- whether in individuals or in states."
elegant: "Your military culture, modeled on the camp, has never cultivated the finest music. You raise your youth collectively like colts in a herd, never training individuals for broader leadership. A person educated for both war and governance would surpass Tyrtaeus's warrior-ideal, honoring courage always as the fourth virtue, not the first."
summary: The Athenian criticizes the Cretan and Spartan educational systems as militaristic and collective, producing soldiers but not statesmen. He argues that the greatest warrior would also be a governor, honoring courage as the fourth virtue rather than the first.
distilled: Military cultures that train youth collectively like herds produce soldiers but not complete leaders, and wrongly elevate courage above the other virtues.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The comparison of Spartan and Cretan youth to colts herded together is both vivid and pointed -- it implies that Dorian education treats humans like animals. The reference to Tyrtaeus, the martial poet of Sparta, continues from Laws 1.629a-630d, where the ranking of courage as fourth among virtues was first established (after wisdom, temperance, and justice). This passage implicitly criticizes the entire Spartan educational model (agoge). Cross-reference: Laws 1.629a-630d on Tyrtaeus and the ranking of virtues; Republic 8.547d-548a on the spirited timocratic character produced by military societies; Aristotle, Politics 2.9 (1271b) on the defects of Spartan education; Xenophon, Lacedaemonian Constitution on the collective raising of Spartan youth.

narrative:
"Once more, Stranger, I must complain that you depreciate our lawgivers."

"Not intentionally, if at all, my good friend; but whither the argument leads, thither let us follow; for if there be indeed some strain of song more beautiful than that of the choruses or the public theatres, I should like to impart it to those who, as we say, are ashamed of these, and want to have the best."

"Certainly."

"When things have an accompanying charm, either the best thing in them is this very charm, or there is some rightness or utility possessed by them; -- for example, I should say that eating and drinking, and the use of food in general, have an accompanying charm which we call pleasure; but that this rightness and utility is just the healthfulness of the things served up to us, which is their true rightness."

"Just so."

"Thus, too, I should say that learning has a certain accompanying charm which is the pleasure; but that the right and the profitable, the good and the noble, are qualities which the truth gives to it."

"Exactly."

"And so in the imitative arts -- if they succeed in making likenesses, and are accompanied by pleasure, may not their works be said to have a charm?"

"Yes."

"But equal proportions, whether of quality or quantity, and not pleasure, speaking generally, would give them truth or rightness."

"Yes."

"Then that only can be rightly judged by the standard of pleasure, which makes or furnishes no utility or truth or likeness, nor on the other hand is productive of any hurtful quality, but exists solely for the sake of the accompanying charm; and the term 'pleasure' is most appropriately applied to it when these other qualities are absent."

"You are speaking of harmless pleasure, are you not?"

"Yes; and this I term amusement, when doing neither harm nor good in any degree worth speaking of."

"Very true."
refashioned:
"Once again, Stranger, I must object that you are disparaging our lawgivers."

"Not intentionally, my friend. But we must follow where the argument leads. If there exists a kind of music more beautiful than what choruses and public theatres offer, I want to share it with those elders who are ashamed of ordinary performance and desire the very best."

"Fair enough."

"When things have an accompanying charm, either that charm is the best thing about them, or they possess some separate rightness or utility. For example, eating and drinking have an accompanying charm we call pleasure, but their true rightness lies in the healthfulness of the food -- that is what makes them genuinely good."

"Exactly."

"Similarly, learning has an accompanying charm -- the pleasure of discovery. But the genuinely right, profitable, good, and noble aspects of learning come from its truth."

"Precisely."

"And in the imitative arts: when they succeed in creating accurate likenesses and are accompanied by pleasure, their works can be said to have charm?"

"Yes."

"But it is accuracy of proportion -- in both quality and quantity -- not pleasure, that gives them truth or rightness?"

"Yes."

"Therefore only those things can be properly judged by the standard of pleasure alone which produce no utility, truth, or accurate likeness, and cause no harm either -- things that exist solely for the sake of the accompanying charm. The term 'pleasure' is most precisely applied when these other qualities are absent."

"You mean harmless pleasure?"

"Yes -- and I call this amusement, when it does neither significant harm nor significant good."

"True."
elegant: "We must follow the argument. In all things, charm accompanies some deeper rightness: food's charm is pleasure, but its rightness is health; learning's charm is enjoyment, but its rightness is truth; imitative art's charm is aesthetic pleasure, but its rightness is accuracy. Only that which produces no utility, truth, likeness, or harm can be judged purely by pleasure. This I call harmless amusement."
summary: After Cleinias objects to criticism of Dorian lawgivers, the Athenian develops a crucial distinction: in food, learning, and art, pleasure is merely the accompanying charm, while the true value lies in health, truth, or accuracy respectively. Only pure amusement -- which produces nothing useful or harmful -- can be judged by pleasure alone.
distilled: Pleasure is the accompanying charm of things whose true value lies elsewhere: health for food, truth for learning, accuracy for art.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. This is one of the Laws' most important aesthetic arguments. The systematic analogy between food/health, learning/truth, and art/accuracy establishes that pleasure is never the primary criterion for evaluating anything of consequence. The category of 'harmless amusement' -- pleasure without utility, truth, or harm -- is the only domain where pleasure can be the sole standard. This implicitly excludes music from the 'amusement' category, since music has profound educational effects. Cross-reference: Gorgias 462b-465d on the parallel between cookery/medicine and rhetoric/justice; Republic 10.601a-602b on the poet's lack of knowledge about what he imitates; Philebus 51b-52b on true and false pleasures; Aristotle, Poetics 1448b on the pleasure of accurate imitation.

narrative: "Then, if such be our principles, we must assert that imitation is not to be judged of by pleasure and false opinion; and this is true of all equality, for the equal is not equal or the symmetrical symmetrical, because somebody thinks or likes something, but they are to be judged of by the standard of truth, and by no other whatever."
refashioned: "Given these principles, we must maintain that imitation cannot be judged by pleasure or false opinion. This applies to all judgments of equality and proportion: things are not equal because someone thinks they are, nor symmetrical because someone finds them pleasing. They must be judged by the standard of truth alone, and by no other criterion."
elegant: "Imitation must be judged by truth, not pleasure or opinion. Equality is not equal because someone thinks so; symmetry is not symmetrical because someone likes it. Truth alone is the standard."
summary: The Athenian states the principle plainly: all imitative art must be judged by truth and accuracy, not by the pleasure it gives or the opinions people hold about it, just as mathematical equality is objective.
distilled: Art, like equality itself, must be judged by the standard of truth alone, not by pleasure or opinion.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage makes the objectivist aesthetic position explicit: truth is the sole criterion for evaluating imitation, just as truth determines mathematical equality. The analogy between art and mathematics reinforces the idea that aesthetic judgments are not subjective. Cross-reference: Republic 10.602c-603b on the unreliability of sense perception and opinion in evaluating art; Philebus 55d-59d on the hierarchy of knowledge, with mathematics as a model of truth; Hippias Major 289d-290d on the difficulty of defining beauty objectively.

narrative:
"Quite true."

"Do we not regard all music as representative and imitative?"

"Certainly."

"Then, when any one says that music is to be judged of by pleasure, his doctrine cannot be admitted; and if there be any music of which pleasure is the criterion, such music is not to be sought out or deemed to have any real excellence, but only that other kind of music which is an imitation of the good."

"Very true."

"And those who seek for the best kind of song and music ought not to seek for that which is pleasant, but for that which is true; and the truth of imitation consists, as we were saying, in rendering the thing imitated according to quantity and quality."

"Certainly."

"And every one will admit that musical compositions are all imitative and representative. Will not poets and spectators and actors all agree in this?"

"They will."

"Surely then he who would judge correctly must know what each composition is; for if he does not know what is the character and meaning of the piece, and what it represents, he will never discern whether the intention is true or false."

"Certainly not."

"And will he who does not know what is true be able to distinguish what is good and bad? My statement is not very clear; but perhaps you will understand me better if I put the matter in another way."

"How?"

"There are ten thousand likenesses of objects of sight?"

"Yes."
refashioned:
"Exactly."

"Don't we consider all music to be representational and imitative?"

"We do."

"Then the doctrine that music should be judged by pleasure cannot stand. Any music judged solely by its pleasantness is not to be valued or considered excellent. Only music that imitates what is good deserves our pursuit."

"Very true."

"Those who seek the best songs and music should look not for what is pleasant but for what is true. And the truth of imitation, as we said, consists in accurately rendering what is imitated in both quantity and quality."

"Certainly."

"Everyone acknowledges that musical compositions are imitative and representational -- poets, audiences, and performers all agree on this?"

"They do."

"Then whoever judges correctly must understand what each composition represents. If he does not know the character, meaning, and subject of a piece, he can never determine whether the artistic intention has been realized truthfully or not."

"Certainly not."

"And can someone who does not know what is true distinguish between good and bad? Let me put this more clearly with an example."

"Please do."

"There are countless likenesses of visible objects in the world?"

"Yes."
elegant: "All music is imitation. Therefore music judged only by pleasure has no real excellence -- only music imitating the good deserves pursuit. The truth of imitation means rendering the subject accurately. To judge correctly, one must know what is being imitated, whether the imitation is truthful, and what is genuinely good. Without this knowledge, no sound judgment is possible."
summary: The Athenian builds the logical chain: all music is imitation; imitation should be judged by truth, not pleasure; therefore judging music by pleasure alone is invalid. Correct judgment requires knowing what is being imitated, whether the imitation is accurate, and what constitutes genuine goodness.
distilled: Since all music is imitation, it must be judged by the truth and accuracy of what it represents, not by the pleasure it produces.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The claim that all music is imitative (mimetic) is foundational to Plato's aesthetics across multiple dialogues. The three requirements for correct judgment -- knowing the subject, knowing truth, and knowing execution -- are formally stated for the first time here and elaborated in 2.42. The transition from abstract principle to visual examples (likenesses of visible objects) prepares the argument in 2.41. Cross-reference: Republic 3.393c-394d and 10.595a-602b on mimesis; Ion 530b-c on the rhapsode's need to understand what he recites; Cratylus 430a-432d on the correctness of names as likenesses; Sophist 235d-236c on the distinction between true and false likenesses.

narrative: "And can he who does not know what the exact object is which is imitated, ever know whether the resemblance is truthfully executed? I mean, for example, whether a statue has the proportions of a body, and the true situation of the parts; what those proportions are, and how the parts fit into one another in due order; also their colours and conformations, or whether this is all confused in the execution: do you think that any one can know about this, who does not know what the animal is which has been imitated?"
refashioned: "Can someone who does not know the actual object being imitated ever determine whether the likeness is truthfully executed? Consider a statue, for example: does it have the correct bodily proportions? Are the parts positioned correctly? Do they fit together in proper relation to each other? Are the colors and shapes accurate, or has the whole thing been botched in execution? Can anyone possibly evaluate these things without knowing what animal or figure is being represented?"
elegant: "Without knowing the original, no one can judge whether an imitation is accurate. Can you evaluate a statue's proportions, positioning, and color without knowing what creature it represents? Impossible."
summary: The Athenian argues through the example of sculpture that judging an imitation's accuracy requires knowledge of the original. Without knowing what a statue is meant to represent, one cannot assess its proportions, positioning, or fidelity.
distilled: You cannot judge whether an imitation is truthful without knowing what original it is supposed to represent.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The example of statuary makes the abstract principle of 2.39-2.40 concrete. The detailed criteria for evaluating a statue -- proportions, positioning of parts, colors, conformations -- reflect genuine Greek art criticism (cf. Polyclitus's Canon on ideal proportions). The argument applies by analogy to music: judging a musical imitation requires knowing what character or disposition it represents. Cross-reference: Republic 10.598a-c on painters creating likenesses without knowledge; Sophist 235d-236c on accurate (eikastic) vs. deceptive (phantastic) imitation; Cratylus 432b-c on what makes a good likeness; Xenophon, Memorabilia 3.10 on Socrates's conversations with sculptors about representing the soul through the body.

narrative:
"Impossible."

"But even if we know that the thing pictured or sculptured is a man, who has received at the hand of the artist all his proper parts and colours and shapes, must we not also know whether the work is beautiful or in any respect deficient in beauty?"

"If this were not required, Stranger, we should all of us be judges of beauty."

"Very true; and may we not say that in everything imitated, whether in drawing, music, or any other art, he who is to be a competent judge must possess three things; -- he must know, in the first place, of what the imitation is; secondly, he must know that it is true; and thirdly, that it has been well executed in words and melodies and rhythms?"

"Certainly."
refashioned:
"Impossible."

"But even if we know the subject is a human being, and the artist has included all the correct parts, colors, and proportions, must we not also evaluate whether the work is beautiful, or whether it falls short of beauty in some respect?"

"If that additional judgment were not required, Stranger, then everyone would be a qualified judge of beauty."

"Exactly. So may we establish that a competent judge of any imitation -- whether in visual art, music, or any other medium -- must possess three kinds of knowledge? First, he must know what is being imitated. Second, he must know whether the imitation is accurate. Third, he must know whether it has been well executed in its particular medium -- in words, melodies, and rhythms."

"Certainly."
elegant: "Even knowing the subject and its accurate reproduction, we must still judge beauty -- otherwise everyone would be a qualified critic. A competent judge of imitation requires three things: knowledge of what is imitated, knowledge of whether it is accurate, and knowledge of whether it is well executed in its medium."
summary: The Athenian establishes the three requirements for competent aesthetic judgment: knowing what is being imitated, knowing whether the imitation is accurate, and knowing whether it has been skillfully executed in its particular medium.
distilled: Competent aesthetic judgment requires knowing what is imitated, whether the imitation is true, and whether it is well executed.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The three-part criterion for aesthetic judgment is one of the most systematic statements of art criticism in ancient philosophy. The three requirements -- identification of subject, truth of representation, and quality of execution -- form a hierarchy: one cannot evaluate truth without knowing the subject, nor execution without knowing truth. Cleinias's observation that 'we should all be judges of beauty' if only recognition were needed is shrewd. Cross-reference: Republic 10.601c-602a on the three arts related to any object (use, making, imitation); Ion 531a-532c on the specific knowledge required for judging different arts; Aristotle, Poetics 1460b on judging imitation by different standards; Plato, Statesman 258d-259d on the distinction between cognitive and practical judgment.

narrative:
"Then let us not faint in discussing the peculiar difficulty of music. Music is more celebrated than any other kind of imitation, and therefore requires the greatest care of them all. For if a man makes a mistake here, he may do himself the greatest injury by welcoming evil dispositions, and the mistake may be very difficult to discern, because the poets are artists very inferior in character to the Muses themselves, who would never fall into the monstrous error of assigning to the words of men the gestures and songs of women; nor after combining the melodies with the gestures of freemen would they add on the rhythms of slaves and men of the baser sort; nor, beginning with the rhythms and gestures of freemen, would they assign to them a melody or words which are of an opposite character; nor would they mix up the voices and sounds of animals and of men and instruments, and every other sort of noise, as if they were all one."

"But human poets are fond of introducing this sort of inconsistent mixture, and so make themselves ridiculous in the eyes of those who, as Orpheus says, 'are ripe for true pleasure.' The experienced see all this confusion, and yet the poets go on and make still further havoc by separating the rhythm and the figure of the dance from the melody, setting bare words to metre, and also separating the melody and the rhythm from the words, using the lyre or the flute alone."
refashioned:
"Let us not shrink from discussing music's unique difficulty. Music is the most celebrated form of imitation and therefore demands the greatest care. A mistake in music can cause the gravest self-harm by cultivating evil dispositions, and such mistakes are extremely hard to detect."

"Consider that poets are far inferior in artistic integrity to the Muses themselves. The Muses would never commit the grotesque error of pairing men's words with women's gestures and songs, or of combining the melodies and gestures of free citizens with the rhythms of slaves and vulgar people. They would never assign to the rhythms of free people a melody or lyrics of opposite character, nor would they jumble together the voices of animals, humans, and instruments as if they were all the same thing."

"But human poets love this kind of incoherent mixing, making themselves ridiculous to those who, as Orpheus puts it, 'are ripe for true pleasure.' Experienced listeners see through the confusion, yet poets go further still -- separating dance rhythm from melody, setting bare words to meter without music, and isolating melody and rhythm from words by playing the lyre or flute alone."
elegant: "Music demands the greatest care because errors in it corrupt character and are hard to detect. The Muses would never mix gendered expressions, blend free and servile styles, or jumble animal and human sounds. But human poets love such incoherent mixing, appearing ridiculous to the discerning. They further damage art by separating its natural unity -- stripping words from melody, rhythm from dance, and playing instruments without song."
summary: The Athenian warns that music is the most dangerous form of imitation because errors corrupt character and are hard to detect. He catalogs the mistakes human poets make -- mixing gendered, social, and species-specific elements, and fragmenting the natural unity of words, melody, rhythm, and dance.
distilled: Music is the most dangerous imitative art because its errors are subtle and corrupting, and human poets constantly fragment and mix elements that should remain unified.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage contains Plato's most detailed critique of contemporary musical practice. The principle of decorum -- that artistic elements should be consistent in character (gender, social class, species) -- reflects conservative Greek aesthetic theory. The critique of separating music's components anticipates the modern debate about 'absolute music' vs. text-based music. The reference to Orpheus as an authority on 'true pleasure' is notable -- Orpheus represents the ideal unity of word, song, and spiritual power. Cross-reference: Republic 3.397a-398b on the simple vs. complex style of imitation; Republic 3.399c-400a on instruments and modes; Laws 7.812d-813a on proper musical composition; Aristophanes, Frogs 1261-1300 for comic criticism of Euripides's musical innovations.

narrative:
"For when there are no words, it is very difficult to recognize the meaning of the harmony and rhythm, or to see that any worthy object is imitated by them. And we must acknowledge that all this sort of thing, which aims only at swiftness and smoothness and a brutish noise, and uses the flute and the lyre not as the mere accompaniments of the dance and song, is exceedingly coarse and tasteless. The use of either instrument, when unaccompanied, leads to every sort of irregularity and trickery."

"This is all rational enough. But we are considering not how our choristers, who are from thirty to fifty years of age, and may be over fifty, are not to use the Muses, but how they are to use them. And the considerations which we have urged seem to show in what way these fifty-year-old choristers who are to sing, may be expected to be better trained. For they need to have a quick perception and knowledge of harmonies and rhythms; otherwise, how can they ever know whether a melody would be rightly sung to the Dorian mode, or to the rhythm which the poet has assigned to it?"
refashioned:
"When music lacks words, it becomes very difficult to discern the meaning of the harmony and rhythm, or to recognize whether anything worthwhile is being represented. We must acknowledge that music aimed solely at speed, smoothness, and raw sound -- using the flute and lyre as independent instruments rather than accompaniments to dance and song -- is exceedingly crude and tasteless. Either instrument played without accompaniment leads to every kind of irregularity and mere showmanship."

"All this is reasonable enough. But our concern is not how to prevent our elder choristers -- those between thirty and fifty, or even older -- from engaging with the Muses. It is how they should properly engage. Our discussion suggests how these fifty-year-old singers can be expected to have superior training. They need sharp perception and thorough knowledge of harmonies and rhythms. Without this, how could they ever determine whether a melody is properly sung in the Dorian mode, or set to the rhythm the poet intended?"
elegant: "Wordless music makes meaning unrecognizable -- mere speed and noise are crude and tasteless. Instruments played alone breed trickery. But our question is how elder choristers should engage with music, not whether they should. They need deep knowledge of harmony and rhythm to judge whether melodies are properly executed in the correct mode."
summary: The Athenian condemns purely instrumental music as crude and meaningless, arguing that words give music its intelligible content. He then pivots to the practical question: elder choristers need deep knowledge of harmony and rhythm to perform and judge music properly.
distilled: Instrumental music without words is crude because it obscures meaning, and elder choristers need deep musical knowledge to perform with proper judgment.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The condemnation of purely instrumental music reflects the Greek priority of song (melos) over instrumental performance (krouma). The criticism of 'swiftness and smoothness' targets virtuosic display for its own sake -- a recurring complaint in Greek musical criticism. The practical turn -- from what to avoid to what to cultivate -- refocuses the argument on the third chorus. The reference to the Dorian mode is significant: it is consistently the mode Plato approves (Republic 3.399a) for its association with courage and temperance. Cross-reference: Republic 3.399c-d on banning the flute; Aristotle, Politics 8.6 (1341a-b) on the flute's unsuitability for education; Laws 7.812d-e on proper use of instruments; Plutarch, On Music for later discussions of the same issues.

narrative:
"Clearly they cannot."

"The many are ridiculous in imagining that they know what is in proper harmony and rhythm, and what is not, when they can only be made to sing and step in rhythm by force; it never occurs to them that they are ignorant of what they are doing. Now every melody is right when it has suitable harmony and rhythm, and wrong when unsuitable."

"That is most certain."

"But can a man who does not know a thing, as we were saying, know that the thing is right?"

"Impossible."
refashioned:
"Obviously not."

"The general public is absurd in thinking they understand proper harmony and rhythm when they can only be made to sing and keep time by force. It never occurs to them that they are ignorant of what they are doing. Yet every melody is correct when it has appropriate harmony and rhythm, and incorrect when it does not."

"That is beyond question."

"But as we established, can someone who does not truly know something know that it is correct?"

"Impossible."
elegant: "The public absurdly thinks it understands harmony and rhythm, yet can only perform by rote. A melody is right when its harmony and rhythm are suitable, wrong when not. And no one can judge correctness without genuine knowledge."
summary: The Athenian mocks the general public for imagining they understand musical propriety when they can only perform mechanically. True judgment requires genuine knowledge, which most people lack -- reinforcing the argument that only trained experts can evaluate music.
distilled: Most people wrongly believe they understand music when they merely perform by rote; true musical judgment requires genuine knowledge.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The critique of the public's self-deception about musical knowledge connects to the broader Socratic theme of 'not knowing that one does not know.' The distinction between mechanical performance (following rhythm by force) and genuine understanding parallels the distinction in Republic 7 between opinion and knowledge. Cross-reference: Republic 7.522c-523a on the difference between mechanical and genuine mathematical understanding; Apology 22c-d on craftsmen who imagine their expertise extends beyond their craft; Meno 97a-98b on the difference between true belief and knowledge; Theaetetus 201c-202d on knowledge requiring logos.

narrative:
"Then now, as would appear, we are making the discovery that our newly-appointed choristers, whom we hereby invite and, although they are their own masters, compel to sing, must be educated to such an extent as to be able to follow the steps of the rhythm and the notes of the song, that they may know the harmonies and rhythms, and be able to select what are suitable for men of their age and character to sing; and may sing them, and have innocent pleasure from their own performance, and also lead younger men to welcome with dutiful delight good dispositions."

"Having such training, they will attain a more accurate knowledge than falls to the lot of the common people, or even of the poets themselves. For the poet need not know the third point, viz., whether the imitation is good or not, though he can hardly help knowing the laws of melody and rhythm. But the aged chorus must know all the three, that they may choose the best, and that which is nearest to the best; for otherwise they will never be able to charm the souls of young men in the way of virtue."

"And now the original design of the argument which was intended to bring eloquent aid to the Chorus of Dionysus, has been accomplished to the best of our ability, and let us see whether we were right: -- I should imagine that a drinking assembly is likely to become more and more tumultuous as the drinking goes on: this, as we were saying at first, will certainly be the case."

"Certainly."

"Every man has a more than natural elevation; his heart is glad within him, and he will say anything and will be restrained by nobody at such a time; he fancies that he is able to rule over himself and all mankind."

"Quite true."
refashioned:
"We are now discovering that our newly appointed elder choristers -- whom we invite and, though they are free men, effectively require to sing -- must be educated well enough to follow rhythm and melody, to understand harmonies and rhythms, and to select songs appropriate for men of their age and character. They should sing with innocent pleasure in their own performance and inspire younger people to embrace virtuous dispositions with genuine delight."

"With this training, they will achieve a more precise knowledge than ordinary people possess, or even the poets themselves. The poet does not need to know whether his imitation is good -- though he must know the principles of melody and rhythm. But the elder chorus must command all three types of knowledge, in order to choose the best compositions or the nearest to the best. Without this, they will never succeed in charming young souls toward virtue."

"With this, our argument in defense of the Chorus of Dionysus is complete, to the best of our ability. Let us verify our earlier claim: as drinking progresses, a gathering inevitably grows more tumultuous."

"Certainly."

"Every drinker gains an inflated sense of himself -- his heart swells with gladness, he says whatever comes to mind, and no one can restrain him. He imagines himself capable of ruling himself and all of humanity."

"Quite true."
elegant:
"The elder choristers must be educated in rhythm, harmony, and moral selection -- knowing not just melody's laws but whether the imitation is genuinely good. This surpasses even the poet's knowledge, since the poet need not judge moral quality. Without all three kinds of knowledge, elders cannot charm youth toward virtue."

"Our defense of the Dionysian chorus is now complete. As predicted, drinking gatherings grow tumultuous, and every drinker imagines himself master of all."
summary: The Athenian concludes that elder choristers need superior knowledge to poets themselves, mastering not only rhythm and harmony but also the moral quality of imitation. With the defense of the Dionysian chorus complete, he returns to the drinking party, confirming that wine produces inflated confidence in drinkers.
distilled: Elder choristers must surpass poets in knowledge, judging not only melody and rhythm but the moral quality of imitation, so they can lead youth toward virtue.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The claim that the elder chorus must know more than poets is remarkable -- it establishes the educated elder citizen, not the professional artist, as the supreme aesthetic authority. The three kinds of knowledge (subject, truth, execution) from 2.42 are here applied practically. The transition back to the drinking-party theme links the two halves of the argument: wine enables the elder chorus, and the elder chorus provides educated moral guidance. Cross-reference: Republic 10.601c-602a on the three arts (use, making, imitation) and the user's superior knowledge; Ion 531a-532c on the rhapsode's lack of genuine knowledge; Laws 2.42 where the three criteria were established; Laws 1.649d-650b on drinking as a test.

narrative:
"Were we not saying that on such occasions the souls of the drinkers become like iron heated in the fire, and grow softer and younger, and are easily moulded by him who knows how to educate and fashion them, just as when they were young, and that this fashioner of them is the same who prescribed for them in the days of their youth, viz., the good legislator; and that he ought to enact laws of the banquet, which, when a man is confident, bold, and impudent, and unwilling to wait his turn and have his share of silence and speech, and drinking and music, will change his character into the opposite -- such laws as will infuse into him a just and noble fear, which will take up arms at the approach of insolence, being that divine fear which we have called reverence and shame?"

"True."
refashioned:
"Were we not saying that in such situations, the drinkers' souls become like iron heated in fire -- growing softer, younger, and easily shaped by someone who knows how to educate and form them, just as they were in youth? And that this shaper is the same person who guided them when they were young -- namely, the good legislator? He should enact laws governing the banquet so that when a man becomes overconfident, brash, and impudent -- refusing to wait his turn, unwilling to share properly in silence, conversation, drinking, and music -- these laws will transform his character into the opposite. They will instill in him a just and noble fear that rises to meet insolence: that divine fear we have called reverence and shame."

"True."
elegant: "Drinkers' souls, like iron in fire, become soft and malleable. The good legislator -- the same who shaped them in youth -- must enact banquet laws that transform overconfidence and impudence into their opposites, instilling the divine fear we call reverence and shame."
summary: The Athenian recapitulates the key insight: wine makes the soul malleable, and the good legislator must use banquet laws to transform the boldness and impudence that wine produces into reverence and shame -- the same divine fear that guided their education in youth.
distilled: Wine softens souls like iron in fire, and banquet laws must channel this malleability toward reverence and shame rather than insolence.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The iron-in-fire metaphor, first introduced in 2.35, reaches its fullest development here. The concept of 'divine fear' (theios phobos) -- identified with reverence (aidos) and shame -- is one of the Laws' most important moral concepts. It represents a positive form of fear that restrains excess, distinct from cowardice. The banquet laws function as a microcosm of all legislation: shaping character through structured social interaction. Cross-reference: Laws 1.647a-649d on divine fear as part of virtue; Laws 2.35 on the iron metaphor; Republic 4.429d-430b on noble fear vs. ignoble fear; Protagoras 322c-d on Zeus giving humans aidos (reverence) and dike (justice).

narrative: "And the guardians of these laws and fellow-workers with them are the calm and sober generals of the drinkers; and without their help there is greater difficulty in fighting against drink than in fighting against enemies when the commander of an army is not himself calm; and he who is unwilling to obey them and the commanders of Dionysiac feasts who are more than sixty years of age, shall suffer a disgrace as great as he who disobeys military leaders, or even greater."
refashioned: "The guardians and enforcers of these banquet laws are the calm, sober overseers of the drinking party. Without their guidance, the battle against the effects of drink is harder than fighting actual enemies without a composed commander. Anyone who refuses to obey them and the directors of Dionysiac festivals -- who must be over sixty years old -- should suffer a disgrace as great as that of disobeying military commanders, or even greater."
elegant: "Sober overseers must command the drinking party, just as calm generals command an army. Fighting drink without sober leadership is harder than fighting enemies without composed commanders. Disobedience to the Dionysiac feast directors, who must be over sixty, should carry disgrace equal to or greater than military insubordination."
summary: The Athenian prescribes that sober overseers over sixty must govern drinking parties, comparing their role to military commanders. Disobedience to these feast directors should be punished as severely as -- or more severely than -- military insubordination.
distilled: Sober overseers over sixty must command drinking parties with the authority of military generals, and disobedience should bring equal or greater disgrace.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The military metaphor -- fighting drink as fighting enemies -- is characteristic of the dialogue's Dorian context, where military language carries the greatest moral weight. The age requirement of sixty for feast directors ensures that the wisest and most self-controlled citizens oversee the proceedings. The claim that disobeying feast directors is worse than disobeying military leaders elevates social-educational discipline above military discipline -- a pointed statement for a Cretan and Spartan audience. Cross-reference: Laws 1.640d-641a on the symposiarch (president of the drinking party); Laws 6.760d-763a on magistrates and their authority; Xenophon, Symposium 1.1-4 on the role of the symposiarch in Greek drinking culture.

narrative:
"Right."

"If, then, drinking and amusement were regulated in this way, would not the companions of our revels be improved? they would part better friends than they were, and not, as now, enemies. Their whole intercourse would be regulated by law and observant of it, and the sober would be the leaders of the drunken."

"I think so too, if drinking were regulated as you propose."

"Let us not then simply censure the gift of Dionysus as bad and unfit to be received into the State. For wine has many excellences, and one pre-eminent one, about which there is a difficulty in speaking to the many, from a fear of their misconceiving and misunderstanding what is said."

"To what do you refer?"
refashioned:
"Right."

"If drinking and entertainment were regulated this way, wouldn't the participants be improved? They would part as better friends than before, not as enemies, which is what often happens now. All social interaction would be governed by law and respect for it, with the sober leading the intoxicated."

"I agree, if drinking were regulated as you propose."

"Then let us not simply condemn the gift of Dionysus as harmful and unfit for the state. Wine has many virtues, and one especially important one that is difficult to explain to the general public for fear of being misunderstood."

"What do you mean?"
elegant: "Regulated drinking would improve participants: they would leave as better friends, not enemies, with the sober leading the drunk under law's guidance. We should not simply condemn wine. It has many virtues, including one supreme virtue that is hard to explain without being misunderstood."
summary: The Athenian argues that properly regulated drinking parties would produce friendship rather than enmity, with sober leaders guiding the intoxicated. He defends wine as having many virtues, including one supreme benefit that is easily misunderstood by the public.
distilled: Properly regulated drinking produces friendship and moral improvement, and wine possesses a supreme virtue that most people would misunderstand.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. The practical benefit -- turning potential enemies into friends -- grounds the theoretical argument in social reality. The 'pre-eminent excellence' of wine that is difficult to explain refers to its power to soften the soul for moral education (the argument of 2.35). The fear of public misunderstanding reflects a genuine concern: advocating for regulated drinking could easily be distorted into advocacy for drunkenness. Cross-reference: Laws 1.637a-638b where Megillus initially criticizes Athenian drinking; Laws 1.649d-650b on wine as a test; Symposium 176a-e on moderate drinking at philosophical gatherings; Xenophon, Symposium 2.24-26 on wine's social benefits.

narrative: "There is a tradition or story, which has somehow crept about the world, that Dionysus was robbed of his wits by his stepmother Hera, and that out of revenge he inspires Bacchic furies and dancing madnesses in others; for which reason he gave men wine. Such traditions concerning the Gods I leave to those who think that they may be safely uttered; I only know that no animal at birth is mature or perfect in intelligence; and in the intermediate period, in which he has not yet acquired his own proper sense, he rages and roars without rhyme or reason; and when he has once got on his legs he jumps about without rhyme or reason; and this, as you will remember, has been already said by us to be the origin of music and gymnastic."
refashioned:
"There is a tradition that has spread throughout the world, claiming that Dionysus was driven mad by his stepmother Hera, and that in revenge he inflicts Bacchic frenzies and dancing madness on others -- which is why he gave humans wine. I leave such stories about the gods to those who think they can be safely told."

"What I do know is this: no animal is born with mature or complete intelligence. In the developmental period before acquiring proper understanding, every creature rages and roars without purpose, and once on its feet, jumps about without order. This, as you will recall, is what we identified as the origin of both music and gymnastic."
elegant: "The myth says Dionysus, maddened by Hera, gave wine to inflict madness on others. I leave such stories to their tellers. What I know is that every creature is born without intelligence and moves chaotically until developing order. This disorder is the origin of music and gymnastic."
summary: The Athenian dismisses the myth that Dionysus gave wine to spread madness, preferring a naturalistic explanation: all creatures are born without intelligence and move chaotically, and the human need to order this chaos gave rise to music and gymnastic.
distilled: The origin of music lies not in divine madness but in the natural human need to bring order to the chaotic movements of immature creatures.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The dismissal of the Dionysus-Hera myth is characteristically Platonic theological criticism -- gods do not harm or seek revenge (cf. Republic 2.379a-380c).
