The naturalistic alternative -- that music arises from ordering innate chaos -- replaces divine madness with divine order as the explanation for art. The return to the starting point of Book 2 (the restlessness of young creatures) creates a circular structure. Cross-reference: Republic 2.379a-380c on gods not causing evil; Laws 2.3 where the same naturalistic origin was first described; Euripides, Bacchae 1-63 for the traditional myth of Dionysus and Hera; Phaedrus 244a-245c on divine madness as beneficial, which stands in interesting tension with the Laws' more rationalist position.

narrative:
"To be sure, I remember."

"And did we not say that the sense of harmony and rhythm sprang from this beginning among men, and that Apollo and the Muses and Dionysus were the Gods whom we had to thank for them?"

"Certainly."

"The other story implied that wine was given man out of revenge, and in order to make him mad; but our present doctrine, on the contrary, is, that wine was given him as a balm, and in order to implant modesty in the soul, and health and strength in the body."

"That, Stranger, is precisely what was said."

"Then half the subject may now be considered to have been discussed; shall we proceed to the consideration of the other half?"

"What is the other half, and how do you divide the subject?"

"The whole choral art is also in our view the whole of education; and of this art, rhythms and harmonies form the part which has to do with the voice."

"Yes."

"The movement of the body has rhythm in common with the movement of the voice, but gesture is peculiar to it, whereas song is simply the movement of the voice."

"Most true."

"And the sound of the voice which reaches and educates the soul, we have ventured to term music."

"We were right."

"And the movement of the body, when regarded as an amusement, we termed dancing; but when extended and pursued with a view to the excellence of the body, this scientific training may be called gymnastic."

"Exactly."

"Music, which was one half of the choral art, may be said to have been completely discussed. Shall we proceed to the other half or not? What would you like?"

"My good friend, when you are talking with a Cretan and Lacedaemonian, and we have discussed music and not gymnastic, what answer are either of us likely to make to such an enquiry?"

"An answer is contained in your question; and I understand and accept what you say not only as an answer, but also as a command to proceed with gymnastic."

"You quite understand me; do as you say."

"I will; and there will not be any difficulty in speaking intelligibly to you about a subject with which both of you are far more familiar than with music."

"There will not."
refashioned:
"Of course I remember."

"And did we not say that humans' sense of harmony and rhythm sprang from this origin, and that we owe them to Apollo, the Muses, and Dionysus?"

"We did."

"The other story claimed wine was given to humanity as revenge, to drive people mad. Our doctrine, on the contrary, holds that wine was given as a remedy -- to cultivate modesty in the soul and health and strength in the body."

"That is precisely what was said, Stranger."

"Then half our subject has been discussed. Shall we proceed to the other half?"

"What is the other half? How do you divide the subject?"

"In our view, the entire choral art constitutes the whole of education. Within this art, rhythms and harmonies constitute the vocal element."

"Yes."

"Bodily movement shares rhythm with the voice, but gesture is unique to the body, while song is purely vocal movement."

"Exactly right."

"The vocal sound that reaches and educates the soul, we have called music."

"Rightly so."

"Bodily movement, when pursued as recreation, we called dancing. When pursued systematically for bodily excellence, it is called gymnastic."

"Precisely."

"Music -- one half of the choral art -- has been thoroughly discussed. Shall we move to the other half?"

"My friend, you are speaking with a Cretan and a Spartan, and we have discussed music but not gymnastic. What answer do you expect from us?"

"Your question contains its own answer. I take it as both a reply and a command to proceed with gymnastic."

"You understand perfectly. Proceed."

"I will, and it should not be difficult to make myself clear on a subject you both know far better than music."

"It will not."
elegant: "Wine was given not for madness but as a remedy -- cultivating modesty in the soul and health in the body. Half of education, the musical half, is now discussed. The other half is gymnastic: bodily movement pursued for physical excellence, as distinct from dancing (movement as recreation). Since we address Cretans and Spartans, they naturally insist on discussing gymnastic -- a subject they know well."
summary: The Athenian reinterprets wine as a remedy rather than a source of madness, then divides the choral art into two halves: music (vocal education of the soul) and gymnastic (bodily training for physical excellence). Having completed the discussion of music, the Cretan and Spartan interlocutors eagerly request the discussion of gymnastic.
distilled: Education divides into music (training the soul through voice) and gymnastic (training the body through movement), and having completed music, the discussion turns to gymnastic.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias and Athenian Stranger. This passage systematically maps the architecture of education: choral art = education; choral art = music + gymnastic; music = rhythm + harmony (soul education through voice); gymnastic = rhythm + gesture (body education through movement); dancing = bodily movement as recreation. This taxonomy is cleaner than the Republic's treatment (2.376e) and represents Plato's mature educational philosophy. The humor of the Cretan and Spartan insisting on gymnastic is characteristically warm -- they are more at home with physical training than with the subtleties of musical theory. Cross-reference: Republic 2.376e on music and gymnastic as the two parts of education; Republic 3.403c-412b on gymnastic education; Laws 7.795d-796e on the detailed gymnastic curriculum; Protagoras 326b-c on the complete education of an Athenian.

narrative:
"Is not the origin of gymnastics, too, to be sought in the tendency to rapid motion which exists in all animals; man, as we were saying, having attained the sense of rhythm, created and invented dancing; and melody arousing and awakening rhythm, both united formed the choral art?"

"Very true."

"And one part of this subject has been already discussed by us, and there still remains another to be discussed?"

"Exactly."

"I have first a final word to add to my discourse about drink, if you will allow me to do so."

"What more have you to say?"
refashioned:
"Isn't the origin of gymnastics also found in the natural tendency toward rapid movement that exists in all animals? As we discussed, humans -- having developed a sense of rhythm -- created and invented dancing. Melody then aroused and complemented rhythm, and together they formed the choral art."

"Very true."

"One part of this subject has been discussed; the other remains?"

"Exactly."

"Before we proceed, I have one final point to add about drinking, if you will allow me."

"What more do you have to say?"
elegant: "Gymnastics originates in the same impulse as music: the natural tendency of all animals toward rapid movement. Humans transformed this into rhythm, then dancing, then -- joined with melody -- the choral art. But before discussing gymnastics, one final word about drinking."
summary: The Athenian traces gymnastics to the same natural impulse as music -- the tendency of all animals toward movement, which humans uniquely organize through rhythm. Before proceeding to gymnastics, he requests permission for a final remark about drinking.
distilled: Gymnastics and music share a common origin in the natural animal impulse toward movement, which humans alone organize through rhythm.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. The unification of music and gymnastics under a common origin (the animal impulse toward motion) is philosophically elegant -- both arts impose order on natural energy. The request to add a final word about drinking before proceeding to gymnastics creates the transition to the comprehensive wine regulations of 2.53, which serve as the capstone of Book 2. Cross-reference: Laws 2.3 on the origin of music in youthful movement; Republic 3.403c-404e on the relationship between music and gymnastic; Aristotle, Politics 8.5 (1339a) on the natural delight in rhythm and harmony.

narrative:
"I should say that if a city seriously means to adopt the practice of drinking under due regulation and with a view to the enforcement of temperance, and in like manner, and on the same principle, will allow of other pleasures, designing to gain the victory over them -- in this way all of them may be used. But if the State makes drinking an amusement only, and whoever likes may drink whenever he likes, and with whom he likes, and add to this any other indulgences, I shall never agree or allow that this city or this man should practise drinking."

"I would go further than the Cretans and Lacedaemonians, and am disposed rather to the law of the Carthaginians, that no one while he is on a campaign should be allowed to taste wine at all, but that he should drink water during all that time, and that in the city no slave, male or female, should ever drink wine; and that no magistrates should drink during their year of office, nor should pilots of vessels or judges while on duty taste wine at all, nor any one who is going to hold a consultation about any matter of importance; nor in the daytime at all, unless in consequence of exercise or as medicine; nor again at night, when any one, either man or woman, is minded to get children."

"There are numberless other cases also in which those who have good sense and good laws ought not to drink wine, so that if what I say is true, no city will need many vineyards. Their husbandry and their way of life in general will follow an appointed order, and their cultivation of the vine will be the most limited and the least common of their employments. And this, Stranger, shall be the crown of my discourse about wine, if you agree."

"Excellent: we agree."
refashioned:
"If a city sincerely intends to regulate drinking for the purpose of cultivating temperance -- and applies the same principle to other pleasures, aiming to master them through controlled exposure -- then all pleasures may be used. But if the state treats drinking merely as entertainment, allowing anyone to drink whenever and with whomever they wish, along with any other indulgence, I will never approve of that city or that person practicing drinking."

"I would go further than the Cretans and Spartans. I prefer the Carthaginian law: no wine at all during military campaigns -- only water. In the city, no slave, male or female, should ever drink wine. No magistrate should drink during their term of office. No ship's pilot or judge should taste wine while on duty. No one attending an important deliberation should drink. No one should drink during the day at all, except after exercise or as medicine. And no one should drink at night when intending to conceive children."

"There are countless other situations where sensible people with good laws should abstain from wine. If I am right, no city will need many vineyards. Agriculture and daily life will follow an orderly plan, and vine cultivation will be the most restricted and least common occupation. And this shall be the conclusion of my argument about wine, if you agree."

"Excellent -- we agree."
elegant: "If a city regulates drinking to cultivate temperance and mastery over pleasure, wine may be used. If it treats drinking as mere entertainment with no rules, never. I would exceed Cretan and Spartan restrictions, following the Carthaginians: no wine during campaigns, for slaves ever, for magistrates during office, for pilots or judges on duty, for anyone before important deliberations, during the day without medical reason, or at night when conceiving children. With such laws, few vineyards would be needed."
summary: The Athenian concludes Book 2's discussion of wine with comprehensive regulations: drinking is permissible only when regulated for temperance. He proposes restrictions exceeding even Cretan and Spartan practice, following the Carthaginian model -- prohibiting wine during campaigns, for slaves, magistrates, judges, pilots on duty, in daytime, and during conception. The result: a city would need very few vineyards.
distilled: Wine is permissible only under strict regulation aimed at temperance; a well-governed city will restrict drinking so extensively that few vineyards are needed.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger and Cleinias. This passage serves as the capstone of the drinking discussion spanning Books 1-2. The Carthaginian reference is notable -- Plato draws on a non-Greek source for legislation, showing the dialogue's comparative scope. The comprehensive list of prohibitions is remarkably detailed and practical, covering military, civic, judicial, navigational, deliberative, and reproductive contexts. The concluding note about vineyards gives the argument an economic dimension, connecting moral legislation to agricultural policy. Cross-reference: Laws 1.637a-641d for the initial discussion of drinking; Laws 6.775b-e on drinking at weddings; Republic 3.398e-399e on temperance in the ideal state; Aristotle, Politics 7.17 (1336a) on alcohol and conception. The Carthaginian law reflects Greek awareness of Punic customs, also noted by Aristotle (Politics 2.11).
